critic_raw:
The design uses a stratified random split on 36 months of sensor data and maintenance records. For equipment failure prediction with a 7-day labeling window, randomly distributing overlapping rolling windows means that windows that immediately precede a failure event (positive labels) and windows that capture the machine's state after the same failure was repaired (negative labels from the same machine) can be distributed across training and test sets. Training on the post-repair state of a machine to predict the pre-failure state of the same machine is temporal leakage.

The baseline logistic regression receives aggregated sensor statistics only (mean, std, min, max, trend from the 100-step window) and no operator logs or context features. The RNN receives the full multi-modal sequence including operator logs and context features (equipment age, maintenance history, shift). The design describes this as 'fair comparison: both models receive preprocessed sensor information and class-weight correction; the RNN has additional capacity to model multi-modal sequences.' But this is not a fair comparison — the baseline is explicitly denied access to operator logs and context features that may contain high-signal information. Any RNN advantage could reflect the information content of operator logs (e.g., a log entry noting 'unusual vibration observed' is directly predictive of failure) rather than the RNN's sequential modeling capability. The hypothesis claims to test multi-modal RNN vs. sensor-only traditional method, and the design confirms this is what it tests — but framing this as a test of modeling capacity is incorrect.

The concatenation strategy for multi-modal input merges 100-step sensor sequences (numeric time series) with 50-step operator log sequences (categorical/text encoded as 50-dimensional vectors) and context features into a single LSTM input. Concatenating sequences of different temporal granularities (1-minute sensor samples vs. discrete event logs) into a single sequence forces the LSTM to learn a shared temporal representation across modalities with different sampling rates and semantic types. This is a non-trivial architectural assumption that the design does not justify or validate.

Section 9 states 'The temporal split automatically accounts for temporal trends and equipment aging because training precedes validation and test temporally,' but the split strategy is random (Section 3: 'A random 15% of observations is held out as the test set'). The temporal ordering claim in Section 9 is inconsistent with the random split description in Section 3.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random splitting of 36-month sensor data distributes post-repair windows and pre-failure windows from the same failure event across training and test sets, creating temporal leakage.

The logistic regression baseline is explicitly denied access to operator logs and context features given to the RNN — the comparison tests information access (sensor-only vs. multi-modal) rather than modeling architecture (logistic regression vs. LSTM).

Concatenating 1-minute sensor sequences with discrete event log sequences into a single LSTM input forces a shared temporal representation across modalities with different sampling rates and semantic types, an unvalidated architectural assumption.
Section 9 claims the temporal split ensures training precedes test temporally, but Section 3 explicitly describes a random 15% holdout — these descriptions are mutually inconsistent.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random splitting of 36-month sensor data distributes post-repair windows and pre-failure windows from the same failure event across training and test sets, creating temporal leakage.

The logistic regression baseline is explicitly denied access to operator logs and context features given to the RNN — the comparison tests information access (sensor-only vs. multi-modal) rather than modeling architecture (logistic regression vs. LSTM).

Concatenating 1-minute sensor sequences with discrete event log sequences into a single LSTM input forces a shared temporal representation across modalities with different sampling rates and semantic rates, an unvalidated architectural assumption.
Section 9 claims the temporal split ensures training precedes test temporally, but Section 3 explicitly describes a random 15% holdout — these descriptions are mutually inconsistent.
